serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of aglio e olio pasta is approximately 300 g, comprising a single plate of spaghetti tossed with olive oil, garlic, shiitake mushrooms, and chilli flakes.

nutrient_source.title: Spaghetti with olive oil, garlic, and mushrooms — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Spaghetti cooked (NDB 20124), Olive oil (NDB 04053), Shiitake mushrooms cooked (NDB 11268), Garlic raw (NDB 11215)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for aglio e olio with shiitake mushrooms. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked spaghetti (~65% by weight), olive oil (~10%), cooked shiitake mushrooms (~20%), and raw garlic (~5%). Proportions are consistent with standard restaurant preparation. Energy cross-checked using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — spaghetti, white, boiled (GI ~46–50)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 48 is based on white spaghetti boiled al dente (GI range 46–50, mean ~48) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Aglio e olio preparation does not materially alter the GI of the pasta base; olive oil and garlic additions may marginally lower the effective GI due to fat-mediated gastric emptying delay. GL per serving calculated as (48 × 73.5 g carbohydrate in 300 g serving) / 100 = 35.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~180 mg/100 g; ~540 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single 300 g restaurant serving contributes approximately 540 mg sodium (~23% of the 2,300 mg daily limit); individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt and monitor portion size, particularly if the dish is prepared with salted pasta water or added seasoning.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~35 per serving) driven by refined wheat pasta
type2_diabetes.guidance: The glycemic load of approximately 35 per standard serving is moderate-to-high for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; substituting whole-wheat or legume-based pasta and controlling portion size to 150–200 g can meaningfully reduce postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly monounsaturated fat from olive oil; low saturated fat and zero cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The olive oil base provides a favourable monounsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio (~5:1), which is consistent with Mediterranean dietary patterns associated with reduced cardiovascular risk; however, total fat and caloric density should be monitored in energy-restricted cardiac rehabilitation diets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aglio e Olio with Shiitake Mushrooms is an Italian-origin pasta dish adapted for the Singapore café dining scene. The dish consists of spaghetti tossed in extra-virgin olive oil with thinly sliced garlic, dried chilli flakes, and sautéed shiitake mushrooms, making it fully vegetarian and vegan-friendly. The preparation involves boiling spaghetti to al dente texture, then finishing it in a pan with fragrant garlic-infused olive oil, umami-rich shiitake mushrooms, and a gentle heat from chilli flakes. The result is a light yet flavourful pasta with a silky, oil-coated texture, mild garlic aroma, and earthy mushroom notes. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~185 kcal/100 g), a useful carbohydrate base from pasta, heart-healthy monounsaturated fats from olive oil, and bioactive compounds including allicin from garlic and lentinan from shiitake mushrooms. The glycemic index is approximately 48 (low-medium), driven by the refined wheat pasta base; the fat content from olive oil moderates gastric emptying and may blunt postprandial glucose rise. Sodium is moderate at approximately 180 mg/100 g and should be monitored by individuals with hypertension. The dish is free from dairy, meat, and shellfish, making it suitable for vegetarian, vegan, and halal diners. It is commonly served as a main course in Western-style cafés and casual dining restaurants in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for aglio e olio with shiitake mushrooms as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted USDA SR Legacy composite of the constituent ingredients (cooked spaghetti, olive oil, shiitake mushrooms, garlic). GI is based on the analogous white spaghetti entry from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient proportions are well-established for this dish type and the USDA SR Legacy data for each component is high quality.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates from USDA SR Legacy; no HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry exists for this specific dish. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: olive oil and shiitake mushrooms contain no industrially produced trans fats; USDA SR Legacy reports 0 g trans fat for these ingredients. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: all ingredients are plant-based; USDA SR Legacy confirms 0 mg cholesterol for olive oil, pasta, mushrooms, and garlic. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; B12 is naturally zero. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy composite for this dish; shiitake mushrooms may contribute trace vitamin D2 but a reliable per-100g composite value cannot be confirmed from the available source data. 6. GI of 48 is based on white spaghetti boiled al dente from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); olive oil addition may marginally lower effective GI. 7. Serving size of 300 g is a standard restaurant plate estimate; actual restaurant portions may vary. 8. The input record incorrectly classified this dish under cuisine 'Coffee' and food_group 'Desserts'; corrected to 'Italian / Western' and 'Mixed' (pasta dishes) respectively. 9. The 🌱 emoji in the original food_name has been removed from the canonical name per Veritas naming rules; vegetarian status is captured in dietary_flags.
